text: The surgeon performs an urgent diagnostic laparoscopy for adhesive intestinal obstruction, entering the abdomen with a Veress needle at Palmer's point. Two working ports are placed on the left flank, and multiple omental adhesions to the anterior abdominal wall are visualized and carefully dissected.

After completing the omental adhesiolysis, the surgeon visualizes the collapsed small bowel loop near the vaginal vault. Flimsy omental adhesions to the small bowel loops are carefully dissected. The left ovary is identified, and omental adhesions to it are also removed. The omentum is repositioned to one side, allowing examination of the distended small bowel. The surgeon traces the bowel proximally and distally to locate the transition zone, using graspers to handle the bowel delicately to avoid perforation.

The surgeon traces the distended small bowel distally and identifies the transition zone at the level of the vaginal vault, where adhesions are causing obstruction. The small bowel adhesions are carefully released, and a hemostatic agent is visible over the vaginal vault, potentially contributing to adhesion formation.

The surgeon encounters a dense fibrotic band of adhesions on the left lateral side of the vaginal vault, likely due to suture repair. Sharp dissection with cold scissors proves challenging due to the thickness of the band. Sutures and a hemostatic agent are visible over the vaginal vault, possibly used to control bleeding during the initial surgery.
The surgeon performs further adhesiolysis to release the small bowel from adhesions around the vaginal vault and the right ovary, tracing the bowel distally up to the ileocecal junction.

The surgeon performs intervowel adhesiolysis using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to release the small bowel, tracing it distally to the IC junction. The collapsed small bowel loops are examined, revealing a coiled loop rather than a stricture. The vaginal vault is inspected for loose sutures. A small indirect left inguinal hernia is identified, and a suture repair is initiated with thin bites of the peritoneum to prevent nerve injury.
